content.text: What Are Root-Only Tasks?

The AWS root user is the identity created when an AWS account is first established. It is the only identity that can perform certain account-level actions — not because these actions require elevated IAM permissions, but because AWS has explicitly made them root-exclusive. No IAM policy, no permission boundary, and no Service Control Policy can grant these capabilities to any other identity.

AWS designed this restriction deliberately. The tasks that require root are irreversible or account-threatening enough that AWS wants to ensure only the account owner — identified by the original email and password combination — can perform them. This is a safety net against even a compromised administrator account enabling catastrophic changes.
Understanding which tasks require root, and how to perform them safely, is foundational knowledge for any AWS practitioner responsible for account security.

attrs.alt: Diagram categorizing AWS root-only tasks into Account Management, Organizations & Marketplace, and S3 MFA Delete, with safe usage pattern steps

content.text: The Official List of Root-Only Tasks

AWS publishes the complete list of root-required actions in the IAM documentation. As of current documentation, the following tasks cannot be performed by any IAM user or role regardless of permissions:
Task
Why Root Is Required
Frequency
Change account email address
The email is the account's primary identity — only the owner can change it
Rare
Change root user password
Self-referential: only the current credentials can change themselves
Annual (rotation)
Enable or disable MFA on the root user
Security control on the master key itself
Once (then keep enabled)
Close the AWS account
Irreversible destruction of all resources
Hopefully never
Change AWS Support plan
Billing impact at the account owner level
Occasionally
Restore admin when all IAM admins are locked out
Break-glass scenario; only root bypasses IAM
Emergency only
Activate IAM user access to Billing console
Billing data defaults to root-only; this toggle opens it to IAM
Once per account
Request to leave an AWS Organization
Structural change to the organization membership
Rare
Register as an AWS Marketplace seller
Legal/financial accountability attached to account owner
Business decision
Enable MFA Delete on S3 versioned buckets
Strongest deletion protection; requires root + MFA token
Security hardening
Enable certain legacy EC2 features
Old features predating IAM; AWS is phasing these out
Very rare
Modify GovCloud account linkage
Government compliance; special account type rules
Specialized use
Account Email and Password Changes

The account email address is used to identify the account in all AWS correspondence, including security alerts, billing notifications, and account recovery. Changing it should be treated like changing the lock on a vault.
Changing the Account Email
Sign in to the AWS console as the root user.
In the top navigation bar, choose your account name, then Account.
In the Account Settings section, choose Edit next to Email Address.
Enter and confirm the new email address. AWS sends a verification link.
Check the new email inbox and click the verification link within 12 hours.
Before changing:
ensure the new email is a shared mailbox (e.g., aws-root@company.com), not a personal address. Confirm it delivers correctly and that multiple people can access it for recovery purposes.
Changing the account email does not affect existing IAM users, roles, or resources. However, if you use email-based MFA verification, update your MFA setup after the change.
Restoring Access When All Admins Are Locked Out

This break-glass scenario happens more often than you'd expect: the sole IAM admin is terminated, an account gets separated from its SSO directory, or all admin password reset tokens expire. The root user is the only path back.
Step-by-Step Recovery
Sign in to the AWS console with the root email and password.
If root MFA is on a lost or inaccessible device, contact AWS Support for MFA reset (requires verification via the account email and phone number).
Navigate to IAM and find an existing admin user, or create a new IAM user.
Attach the AdministratorAccess policy directly to the user (or create a new admin group and add them).
Sign out of root. Sign in as the new IAM admin.
Once IAM access is restored, re-evaluate and remove the root session.

# After signing in as root and restoring IAM admin access,
# verify the new admin can describe IAM state before signing out of root
aws iam get-account-summary  # should return without error

# List all users with attached admin policies (run as the new IAM admin)
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[*].UserName' --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read user; do
    policies=$(aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name "$user" --query 'AttachedPolicies[*].PolicyName' --output text)
    if echo "$policies" | grep -q "AdministratorAccess"; then
        echo "Admin user: $user"
    fi
done
Activating IAM User Access to the Billing Console

By default, only the root user can access the Billing and Cost Management console. This is a security default, not a permanent requirement. Activating IAM billing access is a one-time root-only action that unlocks billing pages for IAM users and roles with appropriate permissions.

Why this requires root: billing information is sensitive (it reveals spending patterns, service usage, and potentially business strategy) and AWS defaults to restricting it to the account owner. Once activated, you control billing access through normal IAM policies.
Activating Billing Access
Sign in as root.
Open Account (top-right menu) and scroll to IAM user and role access to Billing information.
Choose Edit, select Activate IAM Access, and choose Update.
Sign out of root.
Create an IAM policy that grants billing access and attach it to appropriate roles.

The aws-portal:* actions are the legacy billing permissions. AWS is migrating to newer billing actions (billing:GetBillingData, etc.) — check the current IAM documentation for the most recent action names.
Enabling MFA Delete on S3 Buckets

S3 versioning lets you recover from accidental deletes and overwrites. MFA Delete adds a second protection layer: permanently deleting an object version or disabling versioning requires the root user's MFA TOTP code to be included in the API call. This makes it nearly impossible for a compromised IAM credential to destroy your object history.

MFA Delete is the strongest object-level protection AWS offers. Once enabled, even if an attacker has full S3 permissions, they cannot permanently delete versioned objects without the physical MFA device.
Enabling MFA Delete via CLI (Root Only)
This is one of the few AWS CLI operations that must be performed with root credentials, not an assumed role. You also need your current TOTP code from the MFA device registered to root.

# Prerequisites: root access keys (temporary — delete after this operation)
# and a registered MFA device on the root user

# Step 1: Create temporary root access keys
# (Do this in the AWS console under Security credentials for the root user)

# Step 2: Configure a separate CLI profile for root
aws configure --profile root-mfa
# Enter: root access key ID, secret, region, output format

# Step 3: Get your MFA device serial number (ARN)
aws iam list-mfa-devices --profile root-mfa
# Returns: SerialNumber like arn:aws:iam::123456789012:mfa/root-account-mfa-device

# Step 4: Enable MFA Delete (replace values with yours)
TOTP_CODE="123456"    # current 6-digit code from your authenticator
MFA_SERIAL="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:mfa/root-account-mfa-device"
BUCKET_NAME="my-critical-bucket"

aws s3api put-bucket-versioning     --profile root-mfa     --bucket "${BUCKET_NAME}"     --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled,MFADelete=Enabled     --mfa "${MFA_SERIAL} ${TOTP_CODE}"

# Step 5: Verify
aws s3api get-bucket-versioning --bucket "${BUCKET_NAME}"
# Output should show: "MFADelete": "Enabled"

# Step 6: IMMEDIATELY delete the root access keys
aws iam delete-access-key --profile root-mfa     --access-key-id YOUR_KEY_ID

Root access keys are extremely dangerous. Create them only for this operation, use them, and delete them within minutes. Never store root credentials in environment variables, ~/.aws/credentials, or any code repository.
Disabling MFA Delete
If you need to disable MFA Delete (e.g., migrating to a different bucket protection strategy), the same root + MFA requirement applies:

aws s3api put-bucket-versioning     --profile root-mfa     --bucket "${BUCKET_NAME}"     --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled,MFADelete=Disabled     --mfa "${MFA_SERIAL} ${TOTP_CODE}"

# Note: You cannot disable versioning while MFA Delete is enabled.
# You must first disable MFA Delete, then (if desired) disable versioning.
Closing an AWS Account
Closing an account permanently deletes all resources and terminates the account. AWS requires root to perform this action because it is irreversible. Before closing:
Terminate all resources

content: : EC2 instances, RDS databases, S3 buckets (must be empty), etc. AWS will attempt to delete resources, but leaving them risks final billing charges.

content.text: Download all data

content: : CloudWatch logs, RDS snapshots, S3 objects, and any other data you want to keep.

content.text: Transfer domain registrations

content: : If you use Route 53 for domain registration, transfer domains before closure or they will be lost.

content.text: Cancel Reserved Instances and Savings Plans

content: : You may receive a partial refund depending on usage.

content.text: Export CloudTrail logs

content: : If you have compliance requirements, export trail logs to an external system before closing.

content.text: To close: sign in as root, go to Account, scroll to the bottom, and choose Close Account. AWS confirms the decision with a 90-day suspension period during which you can reopen the account before permanent deletion.
Monitoring Root Activity with CloudTrail

Every root user action is recorded in AWS CloudTrail. Because root usage should be extremely rare — ideally zero after initial setup — any root activity in CloudTrail is a high-priority security signal.

# Search CloudTrail for all root user activity in the past 7 days
aws cloudtrail lookup-events     --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root     --start-time $(date -d '7 days ago' --iso-8601=seconds)     --query 'Events[*].{Time:EventTime,Name:EventName,Source:EventSource}'     --output table

# Specifically check for console sign-in events (most important to monitor)
aws cloudtrail lookup-events     --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=ConsoleLogin     --start-time $(date -d '30 days ago' --iso-8601=seconds)     --query 'Events[?Username==`root`].{Time:EventTime,Source:SourceIPAddress}'     --output table
Set up a CloudWatch alarm to alert your security team whenever root logs in. This is a best practice recommended by CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark and AWS Security Hub:

# Create a CloudWatch metric filter for root console login
# (Assumes CloudTrail is already logging to a CloudWatch Logs group)

aws logs put-metric-filter     --log-group-name "aws-cloudtrail-logs"     --filter-name "RootAccountUsage"     --filter-pattern '{ $.userIdentity.type = "Root" && $.eventType = "AwsConsoleSignIn" }'     --metric-transformations         metricName=RootAccountUsageCount,metricNamespace=Security/RootActivity,metricValue=1

# Create an SNS topic for alerts
aws sns create-topic --name root-login-alerts

# Subscribe your security team email
aws sns subscribe     --topic-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:root-login-alerts     --protocol email     --notification-endpoint security-team@example.com

# Create the CloudWatch alarm
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm     --alarm-name "RootAccountUsageAlarm"     --alarm-description "Alert when root account is used"     --metric-name RootAccountUsageCount     --namespace Security/RootActivity     --statistic Sum     --period 60     --threshold 1     --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold     --evaluation-periods 1     --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:root-login-alerts     --treat-missing-data notBreaching
Delegating Root-Adjacent Tasks via AWS Organizations
While AWS Organizations doesn't remove the root-only restriction, it does provide mechanisms to reduce how often root is actually needed:
Delegated Administrator

content: : For tasks like Marketplace seller registration, an organization can designate a specific member account as the Marketplace management account, reducing root logins to the management account.

content.text: AWS Control Tower

content: : Automates account setup so that each new account is pre-configured (IAM billing access enabled, alternate contacts set, CloudTrail active) without requiring multiple root logins across accounts.

content.text: Account Factory for Terraform (AFT)

content: : Runs account customizations via CI/CD pipelines, handling non-root tasks programmatically and reserving root for the truly irreplaceable actions.

content.text: SCP guardrails

content: : Use Service Control Policies to prevent accidental use of root from being effective in member accounts (note: SCPs cannot block the root user of the management account itself).

content.text: Applying an SCP that denies root in member accounts does NOT prevent the root user from bypassing the SCP if they use the account's root console directly — SCPs from the management account apply to member account roots, but not to the management account root itself.
Preparing for Root Access Before You Need It
The worst time to figure out root access logistics is during an emergency. Prepare a documented run-book that your team can follow when root is needed:
Document where the root password is stored (password manager, hardware vault, physical safe)
Document where the MFA device is stored (physical hardware key, who holds it)
Test root login annually — if MFA isn't working, you want to discover this during a drill, not an outage
Maintain the alternate contacts list so AWS can reach the right people for account recovery
For each account in a multi-account setup, record the root email and ensure it is a functional mailbox
Keep a printed copy of account IDs and root emails in physical secure storage
Summary

Root-only tasks in AWS are a deliberately short list: account email and password changes, MFA management, account closure, support plan changes, IAM billing activation, S3 MFA Delete, and a handful of organization and marketplace actions. Every one of these requires the root credential precisely because it is an identity that no IAM policy can replicate — it exists outside the permission model entirely.

The operational posture: treat root like a physical emergency key. It lives in a vault (a password manager with hardware MFA), you know exactly when to use it (one of the tasks above), and every time it is used, CloudTrail records the fact. An unexpected root login is a security incident, not routine administration.
